items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what I have, what’s coming in, what’s going out—and then a plan that doesn’t require me to babysit it every week.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it structured. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?
Client: Salary is 45500 a year, paid monthly. I also have rental income—40400 annually, it comes in monthly. Then interest and dividends: 21400 annually paid annually, and another 11800 annually that hits monthly.

Client: Yep, exactly.
Advisor: Great. Switching to the outflow side—what are your total monthly expenses, all-in, on average?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 5950.
Advisor: Thanks. And within that, how much is debt payments per month—minimums or required payments, total?
Client: Just the mortgage payment—1950 a month.

Client: Total monthly expenses are 5950.
Advisor: Thanks. And within that, how much is debt payments per month—minimums or required payments, total?
Client: Just the mortgage payment—1950 a month.
Advisor: Mortgage only, 1,950 monthly. Can you remind me of the mortgage details—rate, balance, and when it’s scheduled to be paid off?
Client: Rate is 6.68%. Balance outstanding is 47150. Final payment date is 2036-08-07.

Client: I have it at 67350.
Advisor: Okay. Now let’s talk investable assets—roughly what’s the total you’d consider investable across your accounts?
Client: Investable assets total 224600.
Advisor: Thanks. Can we break that down by where it sits—just the buckets and approximate balances?
Client: Sure. I’ve got 109800 in a taxable account held through an insurance company. Then 56750 in a 401k/IRA at a brokerage. There’s 26000 in a bank-type account at a brokerage. And 32050 in private markets at a brokerage.

Client: Rate is 6.68%. Balance outstanding is 47150. Final payment date is 2036-08-07.
Advisor: Understood. And the home value you’re using for planning—what do you have that at right now?
Client: I have it at 67350.
Advisor: Okay. Now let’s talk investable assets—roughly what’s the total you’d consider investable across your accounts?
Client: Investable assets total 224600.

Client: Just the mortgage payment—1950 a month.

Client: Rate is 6.68%. Balance outstanding is 47150. Final payment date is 2036-08-07.
Advisor: Understood. And the home value you’re using for planning—what do you have that at right now?
Client: I have it at 67350.

Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?
Client: Salary is 45500 a year, paid monthly. I also have rental income—40400 annually, it comes in monthly. Then interest and dividends: 21400 annually paid annually, and another 11800 annually that hits monthly.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting those streams. That’s salary, rental, and two interest-and-dividend lines with different payout timing.
Client: Yep, exactly.

Advisor: Hi Patricia—nice to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what I have, what’s coming in, what’s going out—and then a plan that doesn’t require me to babysit it every week.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it structured. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?

Client: Yeah, thankfully. Post-divorce I’ve kept it clean—just the mortgage.
Advisor: Thanks, Patricia. Since you mentioned the divorce—just to make sure our basics are right—what’s your current marital status?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Got it. And household-wise, is it just you at home, or anyone else living with you—partner, roommate, kids?
Client: It’s just me—1 adult.

Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what I have, what’s coming in, what’s going out—and then a plan that doesn’t require me to babysit it every week.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it structured. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?
Client: Salary is 45500 a year, paid monthly. I also have rental income—40400 annually, it comes in monthly. Then interest and dividends: 21400 annually paid annually, and another 11800 annually that hits monthly.

Client: Sure, go ahead.
Advisor: On the tax side, what bracket do you generally plan around right now—what’s the band you’re using?
Client: I use 35%.
Advisor: Got it. And household-wise—earlier you said it’s just you at home. Any dependents at all we should account for—kids or anyone you financially support?
Client: No—number of dependents is 0.

Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what I have, what’s coming in, what’s going out—and then a plan that doesn’t require me to babysit it every week.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it structured. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?
Client: Salary is 45500 a year, paid monthly. I also have rental income—40400 annually, it comes in monthly. Then interest and dividends: 21400 annually paid annually, and another 11800 annually that hits monthly.

Advisor: That makes sense. And just so I’m aligned—when you say moderate, you’re okay with normal ups and downs, but you want downside scenarios spelled out before you commit.
Client: Exactly.
Advisor: Last admin thing, and then I’ll stop peppering you—how are you engaging with an advisor for this plan? Like, are we talking a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And just to keep us consistent with what we already captured today: we’ve got your income streams—salary, rental income, and interest and dividends—plus the accounts and the home value, the mortgage, and the disability policy. Next time we’ll turn that into goals and a timeline.

Advisor: Got it. And household-wise—earlier you said it’s just you at home. Any dependents at all we should account for—kids or anyone you financially support?
Client: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Now risk—when markets get choppy, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or aggressive overall?
Client: Moderate. I’m fine taking some risk for the long term, but I don’t want anything that blows up cash flow.
Advisor: That makes sense. And just so I’m aligned—when you say moderate, you’re okay with normal ups and downs, but you want downside scenarios spelled out before you commit.

Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what I have, what’s coming in, what’s going out—and then a plan that doesn’t require me to babysit it every week.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it structured. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 119100 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, what are the main pieces making that up?
Client: Salary is 45500 a year, paid monthly. I also have rental income—40400 annually, it comes in monthly. Then interest and dividends: 21400 annually paid annually, and another 11800 annually that hits monthly.

Advisor: Cool. Before we wrap, Patricia, I want to make sure I’ve got your profile basics right—just a couple quick ones.
Client: Yep, go for it.
Advisor: First, in terms of your role in the household plan—since it’s just you—would you say you’re the primary decision-maker on the finances?
Client: Yes—household role is primary.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I label things consistently in my notes—are you okay being listed as Client 1 for this household?

Advisor: Thanks. Can we break that down by where it sits—just the buckets and approximate balances?
Client: Sure. I’ve got 109800 in a taxable account held through an insurance company. Then 56750 in a 401k/IRA at a brokerage. There’s 26000 in a bank-type account at a brokerage. And 32050 in private markets at a brokerage.
Advisor: That helps. And separately from investable, you mentioned the primary residence—so that 67350 is the property value bucket, not part of the 224600, right?
Client: Right—67350 is the home value, separate from the 224600 investable total.
Advisor: Great. Last category for today: protection. Do you have any insurance policies we should factor in—life, disability, anything like that?
